coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of multicolour fluorescence carbon quantum dots from sugarcane waste: Investigation of mercury (II) ion sensing, and bio-imaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kasinathan, Kasirajan
Samayanan, Selvam
Marimuthu, Karunakaran
Yim, Jin-Heong
coredata.dc:description: Initial identification is important in the predictive strategy of many life-threatening ailments, such as infectious diseases and cancer. These diseases' mortality rates could be significantly reduced if they were detected in the early stages of disease development. Natural carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial have sparked the interest of researchers for many years in biomedical applications. The eco-friendly and cost-effective hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent CQDs utilizing sugarcane baggage as a carbon source is discussed in this study. The RAMAN and XRD analysis confirmed the graphitic nature of CQDs. The FTIR, and XPS demonstrate the presence of functional groups such as CO, CC, and CN, hence it's regarded as CQDs. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) findings exhibiting that the synthesized CQDs are predominantly monodispersed spherical in shape, with particle sizes ranging from 2 to 8 nm. It is used to develop a more environmentally friendly and cost-effective fluorescent probe for detecting Hg2+ ions. MCF-7 cells were used to test cancer cell lines, which showed significant bio-labeling capacity and low cytotoxicity. The cell viabilities of the MCF-7 cells were assessed. As a result of their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, we used the synthesized CQDs as an efficient fluorescent probe for multicolour live-cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sugarcane baggage
Carbon quantum dots
Hg2+ ion sensing
MCF-7 Cancer cell

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CA and SB derived CQDs
Structural, optical and functional characterization of CQDs
Surface morphology and elemental mapping analysis of CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2019R1I1A3A01054826).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of CQDs.

(a) XRD pattern (b) UV–visible absorption spectra (Insets show photographs of the CQDs in aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right) (c) FTIR spectra and (d) Raman spectra of CQDs.
(a and b) SEM images of CQDs with different magnifications (c–e) Elemental mapping images of CQDs and (f) EDX spectrum of the synthesized CQDs.
HR-TEM images of CQDs with different magnifications (a) 10 nm, (b) 5 nm, and (c) 2 nm and (d) SAED pattern of CQDs.
PL spectra of synthesized CQDs using different excitation wavelengths with a 20 nm increment from 300 to 440 nm.
(a) XPS survey spectra of CQDs and the high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.

(a) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity. (b) Fluorescent quenching of CQDs in the presence of different metal ions (Ag2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ba2+,Cr3+, and Cd2+). (c) Fluorescent quenching of CQDs in the presence of Hg2+ ions concentration varying from 0 to 300 μM. (d) The linear relationship between F/F0 and 0 to 300 μM concentration of Hg2+ ions with and without CA and inset image shows 25–100 μM concentrations of Hg2+ ions.
The sensing mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CQDs-Hg2+ complexes.
The PL lifetime decays of CQDs in the presence and absence of Hg2+.
MTT assay for cell viability of MCF-7 cells after 24 h incubation with CQDs at various concentrations (6.25–100 μg/mL).
In vitro confocal microscopy images of MCF-7 cells treated with CQDs (a) Bright-field images (b) blue (c) green, and (d) red at excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 555 nm.
Summary of CQD-based sensor for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of multicolour fluorescence carbon quantum dots from sugarcane waste: Investigation of mercury (II) ion sensing, and bio-imaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Advanced Materials Engineering, Kongju National University, Budaedong 275, Seobuk-gu, Cheonan-si, Chungnam 31080, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Advanced Materials Engineering, Kongju National University, Budaedong 275, Seobuk-gu, Cheonan-si, Chungnam 31080, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Thin Film and Nano Science Research Lab, Department of Physics, Alagappa Government Arts College, Karaikudi 630 003, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Thin Film and Nano Science Research Lab

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Thin Film and Nano Science Research Lab, Department of Physics, Alagappa Government Arts College, Karaikudi 630 003, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Initial identification is important in the predictive strategy of many life-threatening ailments, such as infectious diseases and cancer. These diseases' mortality rates could be significantly reduced if they were detected in the early stages of disease development. Natural carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial have sparked the interest of researchers for many years in biomedical applications. The eco-friendly and cost-effective hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent CQDs utilizing sugarcane baggage as a carbon source is discussed in this study. The RAMAN and XRD analysis confirmed the graphitic nature of CQDs. The FTIR, and XPS demonstrate the presence of functional groups such as CO, CC, and CN, hence it's regarded as CQDs. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) findings exhibiting that the synthesized CQDs are predominantly monodispersed spherical in shape, with particle sizes ranging from 2 to 8 nm. It is used to develop a more environmentally friendly and cost-effective fluorescent probe for detecting Hg2+ ions. MCF-7 cells were used to test cancer cell lines, which showed significant bio-labeling capacity and low cytotoxicity. The cell viabilities of the MCF-7 cells were assessed. As a result of their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, we used the synthesized CQDs as an efficient fluorescent probe for multicolour live-cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: In the modern world of nanotechnology, the transformation of agricultural wastes into new nanomaterials for a wide range of applications is a successful approach of ‘waste to wealth.’ Over the last decade, developing countries have consistently faced the problem of soil and earth pollution as a major issue [1–3]. One of the most major pollutants in the ecosystem has been identified as biomass wastes, such as agricultural residues and residential trash from various farming or food processing industrial processes. The researchers have now synthesized CDs from natural solid waste resources such as orange peel, coffee grounds, sugarcane, watermelon peel, cabbage, tamarind, pomelo peel, pineapple peel, lemon peel, papaya juice, and potato using green chemistry ideas. Sugarcane bagasse is one of the most significant biomass wastes generated globally every year [4–10]. After Brazil, India is the world's second largest producer of sugarcane, with 350 Megatonne (Mt) produced. Sugarcane cultivation produces about 40–50% bagasse (dry pulp and fibrous residue) each year, resulting in severe land and water pollution that demands necessitates solid waste management [11,12]. We presented an alternate approach to minimizing soil pollution and contamination by creating carbon compounds from agricultural solid waste. Several new studies on the preparation and characterization of carbon-based nanomaterials derived from solid wastes have recently been published.

Carbon-based materials are a significant role in the development of material science. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm made up of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [13,14]. CQDs were discovered in 2004 while purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes. They have exciting characteristics such as excitation-dependent/independent fluorescence, multicolour emission, excellent dispersibility, and solubility in aqueous and organic solvents [15]. CQDs a new rising star in the carbon family have sparked the interest of researchers due to their remarkable and tunable photoluminescence (PL), high quantum yield (QY), low toxicity, small size, considerable biocompatibility, and low cost. It has significant applications in a broad range of fields, including biomedicine, catalysis, sensors, and optoelectronic devices [16–19]. The synthesized CQDs reveal the elemental composition of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O). Laser ablation, arc discharge, hydrothermal, electrochemical, ultrasonic, solvothermal, and microwave processes are a few of the methods used to make CQDs. When compared to other technologies, the hydrothermal method is a simple, environmentally friendly, one-step approach with a low cost. Because no pollutants are emitted into the atmosphere, carbonization uses the hydrothermal process at low temperatures and self-created pressure [20–22].

The fluorescent sensors based on CQDs provide a new method for detecting heavy metal ions that cause environmental and health risks. Toxic heavy metal ion-containing effluents from many origins, mainly factories, pollute not only surface water but also groundwater, posing a major hazard to clean drinking water and living beings [23–25]. Some heavy metals, such as zinc (Zn2+) or iron (Fe2+), are required for human metabolism and are rarely harmful to human health when present in adequate concentrations. In contrast, other metals, such as mercury (Hg2+), lead (Pb2+), and cadmium (Cd2+) are toxic to humans even in tiny quantities [26,27]. Among heavy metal ions, Hg2+ is one of the most frequent dangerous metal ions in the atmosphere, and its exposure can induce major health issues such as kidney, liver, and nervous system damage. According to the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the maximum Hg2+ level allowed in food and drinking water is 0.002 ppm [28–30]. The need for simple and precise investigative techniques for identifying Hg2+ concentration and pollution potential is rising. As a result, selective monitoring of Hg2+ is critical, especially at low concentrations in the aqueous systems.

Apart from metal ion detection, real-time bio-imaging of living systems in the ecosystem is yet another key application of this CQDs. Cancer is already a major public-health challenge around the world. However, treatment options are limited, and in many cases, accurate diagnosis and therapy are difficult. As a result, enormous efforts in biomedical research have been devoted to developing new approaches for cancer detection, diagnosis, and therapy at an early stage. Nowadays, Cancer nanomedicine is a new branch of nanotechnology that is playing an important role in cancer management by developing new ideas and strategies for cancer treatment [31–34]. To date, various researchers have synthesized and reported on carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNT), fullerenes, graphene, graphene oxides (GO), carbon diamonds, and carbon quantum dots for use in biomedical applications [35–37]. The CQDs are an excellent candidate for cell imaging because they exhibit broad absorption and emission in the deep ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared ranges, and effective multi-photon up-conversion emission. CQDs are free of heavy metals, resulting in better biocompatibility and lower toxicity when compared to traditional QDs. So, CQDs have been used in biomedicine as antibacterial, anticancer, and wound healing agents, as well as for bio-imaging [38–42]. CQDs made of carbon materials have lower toxicity toward organisms and the environment than metal nanoparticles (NPs). The fluorescent cell imaging approach is crucial for early tumour detection in diagnostics because it allows for non-invasive, extremely sensitive, and detailed observation of pathological and physiological activities.

In this study, we prepared highly fluorescent CQDs through a hydrothermal method using the agricultural waste species of sugarcane. The structural, surface morphology with chemical composition, optical property, metal detection, and cytotoxicity of CQDs are all investigated, and the findings are discussed in detail. The prepared CQDs were tested for Hg2+ ion sensing and cell imaging in the MCF-7 cancer cell line, and findings indicate that the promising for sensing and biomedical applications.

The sugarcane bagasse (SB) is obtained from sugarcane juice vendors in the local areas of the Ramanathapuram district of Tamil Nadu (India) for this research. The obtained SB was washed with deionized water. All of the samples were air and sun-dried for up to seven days and being sliced into tiny pieces of 1-mm parts. Citric acid (CA: C6H8O7) and aqueous ammonia were bought from Sigma Aldrich (99.99%). All chemicals were used of analytical reagent (AR) grade and millipore water was used during the study.

The typical experimental procedure was described as follows: firstly, 2 g bagasse was added to 50 ml of deionized water and stirred to produce a homogeneous solution. Next, the aqueous ammonia was applied drop-wise to the precursor solution. Afterward, the homogeneous solution shifted to the stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 12 h. The mixture was cooled to room temperature and then a black color precipitate was formed. The reactive mixture solution was sonicated for 1 h and centrifuged for 30 min at 5000 rpm to remove superior undissolved particles. Then, the supernatant solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm pore diameter microporous membrane to remove the large precipitates. Finally, the bagasse-derived CQDs were obtained and saved for further characterization and application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CA and SB derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized by sugarcane industrial wastes using the hydrothermal method reported by S. Pandiyan and colleagues [43]. The details of the CQDs synthesis protocol are illustrated in Fig. 1 . Pre-treated SB (2 g) and CA is dissolved in 50 ml ultrapure water and stirred to produce a homogeneous solution. Then, the aqueous ammonia was applied drop-wise to the precursor solution. Afterward, the homogeneous solution shifted to the stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 12 h. The mixture was cooled to room temperature and then a black colour precipitate was formed. The reactive mixture solution was sonicated for 1 h and centrifuged for 30 min at 5000 rpm to remove superior undissolved particles. Then, the solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm pore diameter microporous membrane to remove the large precipitates. This gives a clear golden brownish solution. The obtained CA and SB derived CQDs were kept in a refrigerator at 5 °C for additional measurements [1,36].

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired with the help of a (PAN analytical XPERT PRO MPD) diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (=0.15418 nm) as an X-ray source. The UV–visible absorption spectra of CQDs were examined at room temperature using a Shimadzu (UV-1800) spectrophotometer with an absorption wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The FTIR spectra of (Bruker, Model: ALPHA II) CQDs at a frequency range of 4000–400 cm−1 were used to characterize the functional group of these samples. Raman spectrum was recorded using Horiba-Jobin, Lab Ram HR spectrometer. To investigate the surface morphology and composition of the sample, a Zeiss Supra55 VP FESEM/EDX (Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy/Energy-dispersive X-ray) was used. To further confirm the morphologies of the CQDs were examined by HR-TEM on a JEM-2100 microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed on a Shimadzu RF–5301 spectrofluorimeter. The XPS spectra of the produced CQDs were measured using an X-Ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (model: ESCALAB Xi+).

In this study, quantum yield (QY) of the obtained CQDs were measured using standard procedure. The QY of the CQDs was calculated through the following equation:
(1)
φ x
=
φ
std
+
I x
I
std
×
A
std
A x
×
η
x
2
η
std
2
where φ x is the fluorescence quantum yield; x represents the samples. std is the reference compound. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (φstd = 54%) was chosen as the reference. η is the refractive index (1.33 for aqueous solution). A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength. I is the integrated fluorescence intensity under the fluorescence emission spectrum.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm using an aqueous solution of 2 ml CQDs (120 g/mL) completely mixed with various concentration of metal ions solution and left for 1 min. At the same excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission intensities of all metal ion sensing tests were recorded. The fluorescence-quenching extent of CQDs is given by F/F0, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the solution without Hg2+ and F is the fluorescence intensity after Hg2+ addition. All of the experiments were carried out at room temperature.

For the biocompatibility study, kidney epithelial (Vero cells) were procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India. In this study, the MTT assay was used to assess the cell viability of synthesized CQDs against Vero cells. Individual Vero cells were cultured in 75 cm2 cell culture flasks at 37C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere being implanted into 96 well plates (1 × 104/well) and bred for 24 h. After that, the cells were cultured for 24 h with different doses of CQDs (6.25 μg/mL–100 μg/mL). Then, in a humid condition, 10 μL of MTT solvent (5 mg/ml) was added to each well, and the bred time was increased for 4 h. The development of formazan crystals was dissolved by adding 100 μL of DMSO to the solution. The cell viability (%) was calculated using the Eq. (2),
(2)
% C e l l v i a b i l i t y =
A
570 n m T e s t e d C e l l s
A
570 n m
C o n t r o l C e l l s
× 100

Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM) was used to image the MCF-7 cell line. MCF7 cells were cultured in 6-well culture plates at a density of 105 cells per well in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. After that, the cells were incubated with CQDs until they achieved a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. After a 24 h incubation period, the cells were properly rinsed with PBS (pH 7.0), treated with 2.7% formaldehyde (15 min), and rinsed three times with PBS. Eventually, using a “Zeiss LSM 510 Meta laser confocal Microscopy with excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 555 nm, the images were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, optical and functional characterization of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The yield percentage was calculated and compared from the recent reported values, in Table S1 indicates the obtained CQD and yield percentage. The baggage without CA is exhibited 9.1% of yield and the CA mediated yield is 14.5%, from the obtained comparative study the citric acid may influence in the product yield. In addition, it is clearly indicating the yield percentage may vary from every sources of raw material quantity. In concert, the cabbage (5 g) derived CQD exhibited 7.1%, in our reported SB calculated percentage is 9.1% at 2 g of raw material quantity. Fig. S5 shows the SB derived with and without using citric acid CQDs. The only sugarcane derived CQDs intensity is low compared to the citric acid used CQDs. The QY of the SB derived CQDs is 3.34% and the CA and SB derived CQDs is 14.12%. Previously published literature on carbon dots with quantum yield from various precursors and synthesis methods was compared in Table S2. It means that adding citric acid may be enhancing the intensity and product yield of the CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and elemental mapping analysis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The scanning electron microscope (SEM) is a widely utilized method for identifying the size distribution and shape of nanomaterials. The SEM images of synthesized CQDs with different magnification are displayed in Fig. 3 (a and b). The particles were observed to be similar in shape, with the majority of them being spherical or oval. Elemental mapping of the synthesized CQDs was evaluated in order to explore a homogeneous scatter of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) compounds contained in SB, and are displayed in Fig. 3(c–e). The material compositions of the CQDs have been predicted using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). As illustrated in Fig. 3(f), the EDX depicts the estimated distribution of the chemicals found in the CQDs. The existence of C (71.36%), N (07.46%), and O (21.18%) components in SB was detected using this spectrum.

The morphological exploration of prepared CQDs was analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The HR-TEM images of synthesized CQDs with different magnification are displayed in Fig. 4 (a–c). As can be seen from the TEM image in Fig. 4, the CQDs are mostly monodispersed spherical in shape and the particle sizes are the ranging from 2 to 8 nm. The images clearly display that the d- spacing of CQDs is 0.32 nm, as determined by HR-TEM results. This finding well coincides with the XRD pattern. Furthermore, supporting information Fig. S1(a-b) shows the uniform distributions of the particles with different surface areas at 20 nm magnifications. The selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 4 (d). The SAED pattern is assigned to a single phase of graphite it consists of one diffraction ring that could be assigned to the graphene planes (002). The CQDs ring pattern is broad due to small grain size or low crystallinity.

A fluorescence evaluation was carried on the optical properties of the as-synthesized CQDs at various wavelength excitation (300–440 nm) is displayed in Fig. 5 . The increase in the fluorescence emission intensity up to 360 nm and then decreased gradually. The highest emission intensity at 480 nm was observed at 360 excitation wavelength, which suggesting that the SB was effectively carbonized. Because of their quantum effect, larger surface area, and emissive traps, the luminescence spectrum confirms the blue-fluorescence nature of CQDs [52,53]. The red shift in emission wavelength is due to variation in size. As the CA solution is applied to the CQDs, it has a noticeable effect on their fluorescence nature.

The XPS testing may be used to determine the elemental oxidation states, composition, and structure of CQDs. The three high peaks at 285.0, 400.0, and 532.0 eV in the XPS spectrum of CQDs displayed in Fig. 6 (a) are due to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. These findings suggest that the fabricated CQDs are mostly composed of carbon (74.25 %), nitrogen (7.08 %), and oxygen (18.67 %). The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 6b) were integrated into characteristic peaks at 284.2, 285.6, 287.5, and 288.3 eV, relating to CC (sp3 hybridized carbon), CN/CO, COH, and CN/CO. The high-resolution spectrum for N1s (Fig. 6c) revealed a sharp peak at 399.1 and 400.5 eV, which could be attributed to CNC and NH, respectively. The signature peaks in the O1s (Fig. 6d) XPS spectrum of CQDs are at 532.4 eV and 536.3 eV, relating to the CO bond and the CO bond, respectively [54,55].

The selectivity of CQDs has been tested in the presence of various metal ions, including (Ag2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Ba2+,Cr3+, and Cd2+), and the results are shown in Fig. S2. After adding various metal ions to the CQDs solutions, the PL spectra changed significantly toward Hg2+ ions only. The binding of Hg2+ ions significantly reduced the ratiometric PL intensity of CQDs-Hg2+ compared to the free state or in the presence of other metal cations (Fig. S2). The fluorescent behavior of CQDs was tested against different pH environments in order to apply the fluorescent biomarker in a variety of biological environments. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was investigated between pH values of 2–12. The fluorescent intensity was quenched by both acidic and basic pH, but it was dramatically reduced at strong basic pH of 10–12 (Fig. 7 a & S3). The maximum fluorescent intensity was obtained in the pH range 7, which closely matches the physiological environment; further, it can be successfully applied in bio-imaging and sensors. The fluorescent sensing for metal ions was investigated by monitoring the changes in fluorescence intensity as different metal ions were added (Fig. 7b). According to Fig. 7b, Hg2+ ions have the highest fluorescent quenching effect. The highest fluorescent quenching effect was obtained for Hg2+ ions, and CQDs are more selective and sensitive to the detection of Hg2+ ions compared to other metal ions. It has been shown that the hydroxyl and amine groups on CQDs can easily coordinate with Hg2+, resulting in the high selectivity of Hg2+ ions. Fig. 7(c) shows the various effects of Hg2+ at concentrations ranging from 0 to 300 μM and the fluorescence intensity is clearly quenched in a concentration-dependent manner with increasing levels of Hg2+ ions. Fig. 7d shows the linear relationship between F/F0 and 0–300 μM concentrations of Hg2+ ions and the inset image shows 25–100 μM concentrations of Hg2+ ions. The plot of F/F0 with the concentration of Hg2+ ions ranging from 0 to 300 μM showed good linearity with a regression value around 0.996, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs in the absence and presence of metal ions at 475 nm. This linearity meets the basic condition of detecting Hg2+ ions, and the CQDs-based detection method also provides a blue, reliable, and strong fluorescence probe for Hg2+ ion monitoring. The quenching effect of CQDs prepared using CA was greater than that of CQDs prepared without CA at 300 μM (Fig. 7d). The experiment was carried out in triplicate and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated according to the following equation [56,57], ,
(3)
L O D = 3 ×
Standard
deviation
Slope

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated to be 0.1 μM and 0.4 μM, respectively. The detection limit is estimated to be 0.1 μM, which is significantly lower than previously reported values (shown in Table 1 ).

Most of the CQDs are sensitive to metal ions because of their numerous abundant functional groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl) on the surface [64,65]. When Hg2+ ions were added to the prepared CQDs aqueous solution, the fluorescence emission intensity was significantly quenched. This strong quenching effect may originate from the formation of CQDs- Hg2+ complexes. The mechanism of selective sensing of Hg2+ ions using CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 8 . The main recognition mechanism reported in CQD-based systems for Hg2+ detection is the electrostatic interaction between Hg2+ ions and CQDs functional groups. The presence of hydrophilic functionalities such as hydroxyl, amine, and carboxylic in CQDs was confirmed from XPS and FTIR spectroscopies. These functional groups could form a complex with the Hg2+ ions. Therefore, the electron/energy transfer (ET) between Hg2+ ions and CQDs (nonradiative energy/ET) takes place and thus the FL of CQDs is quenched [66–68]. The common mechanism for specific sensing of heavy metal ions such as Hg2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ ions has previously been demonstrated as fluorescence quenching of CQDs [69–71].

The CQDs and CQDs- Hg2+ ions fluorescence decay was measured and interpreted in Fig. 9 . The lifetimes of bare CQDs were found to be very short, as shown by the blue line in the figure, implying a fast exciting recombination process with an average lifetime of 0.48 ns. Furthermore, the decay processes investigated with Hg2+ ions (red line) revealed a significant increase in the decay component, which holds with an average lifetime of 1.06 ns. These findings demonstrated that the addition of Hg2+ ions reduced the fluorescence intensity.

Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity is critical factor in determining whether nanomaterials can be successfully administered for biological applications such as bio-imaging and theranostics [72]. Synthesized CQDs were evaluated for biocompatibility on normal cell lines before being used in bioimaging. Kidney epithelial cells (Vero cells) were employed as normal cell lines, whereas human breast cells (MCF-7) were used as cancer cell models. The viability of Vero cells was tested after they were mixed with CQDs ranging from (6.25–100 μg/mL). The cytotoxicity of CQDs was investigated using a standard MTT assay, as shown in Fig. 10 . As indicated in Fig. 10, the viability of cancer cells treated with CQDs in the range 6.25–100 μg/mL revealed no significant cytotoxicity, indicating that CQDs are biocompatible. The cell viability values for CQDs in the 6.25–100 μg/mL range are 96.16%, 89.52%, 84.91%, 80.64% and 74.86%, respectively. Therefore, CQDs products are non-cytotoxic; they are excellent candidates for biomedical applications.

To validate the imaging application of CQDs, we performed an in vitro bioimaging study using MCF-7 cells. The MCF-7 cells treated with CQDs and examined with a fluorescence microscope display significant fluorescence within the cells, indicating that CQDs are taken up by the cells (Fig. 11 ). When MCF-7 cells were imaged under a microscope with different excitation wavelengths, they became brightly illuminated blue, green, and red. The images were captured using blue, green, and red channels of microscopy with excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 555 nm. There was no morphological damage to the cells, is further confirming the CQDs low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility. These findings revealed that the generated CQDs could be used for cell labeling, biosensors, drug delivery, and other biological applications.

In summary, we have produced CQDs from agricultural waste using eco-friendly, rapid carbonization and hydrothermal processes without the use of oxidizing agents. SB provided as our carbon source. The obtained CQDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, good water solubility, larger size, and modest fluorescence properties. The graphitic nature of CQDs was confirmed by RAMAN and XRD analysis. The presence of functional groups such as CO, CC, and CN is demonstrated by FTIR and XPS, confirming the presence of CQDs. HR-TEM results show that the prepared CQDs are spherical in shape, with particle sizes ranging from 2 to 8 nm. The as-synthesized CQDs have significant optical properties such as high quantum yield, blue fluorescence, and excellent stable fluorescence properties. The prepared CQDs are used as a biocompatible fluorescent sensor for the sequential detection of Hg2+. Among the various metal ions, Hg2+ can selectively quench the fluorescence of CQDs. Furthermore, CQDs could be used as a bioimaging agent for MCF-7 cancer cells due to its excellent biocompatibility and strong luminescence in aqueous solution. This report generates newly designed CQDs from a natural product with diverse applications, which will lead to a highly potential breakthrough in biosensors and bioimaging in the future, as well as a new pathway for biomass waste disposal for long-term use.

Kasirajan Kasinathan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Selvam Samayanan: Resources, Formal analysis, Methodology. Karunakaran Marimuthu: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Jin-Heong Yim: Validation, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2019R1I1A3A01054826).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.154266.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse pulp

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Thambiraj, Ravi Shankaran, Green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse pulp, Applied Surface Science 390 (2016), 435-443.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Boobalan, M. Sethupathi, N. Sengottuvelan, Ponnuchamy Kumar, P. Balaji, Balázs Gulyás, Parasuraman Padmanabhan, Subramanian Tamil Selvan, A. Arun, Mushroom-derived carbon dots for toxic metal ion detection and as antibacterial and anticancer agents, ACS Applied Nano Materials 3, no. 6 (2020), 5910-5919.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sonochemical-assisted green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from crab shell as targeted nanoprobes for cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Khalilalrahman Dehvari, Kai Yi Liu, Po-Jen Tseng, Gangaraju Gedda, Wubshet Mekonnen Girma, Jia-Yaw Chang, Sonochemical-assisted green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from crab shell as targeted nanoprobes for cell imaging, Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers 95 (2019), 495-503.
